text: The surgeon uses a conical stem for the hip arthroplasty through a posterior approach. A checkpoint is placed on the greater trochanter, and a ray is anchored with three Stein pins into the iliac crest percutaneously. The stabilizer is positioned on the iliac crest for acetabular orientation. The baseline limb length and offset are checked, and the lesser to center distance is measured using the robotic system after hip dislocation.

The surgeon performs soft tissue releases to optimize acetabular exposure, removing redundant acetabular labrum amidst scarring from a prior pelvic osteotomy. A checkpoint is placed on the pelvis, followed by registration of the checkpoint and three divots on the stabilizer for acetabular orientation. Points are taken inside the acetabular concavity and around its periphery to register the acetabular bone, confirmed by popping bubbles to match the native anatomy. The setup is prepared for robotic assistance.

The surgeon uses a robotic arm to perform an initial reaming of the acetabulum with a reamer two millimeters smaller than the final target size. Due to hip dysplasia, care is taken to prevent the reamer from skipping. After establishing the desired center of rotation, the plan is adjusted to ream two millimeters larger to achieve a concentric ream. The multi-hole component is then positioned using the robotic arm, ensuring proper inclination and aversion, resulting in an excellent press fit.

The surgeon takes five points around the margin of the acetabular component to assess the achieved version, comparing it to the preoperative plan. Multiple screws are used for fixation. Attention is then turned to the femur, where a box osteotome is used laterally, and the femoral diaphysis is reamed for a conical implant. The stem is dialed into less anteversion than the patient's anatomy, achieving about fifteen degrees of anteversion. The lesser-to-center distance is measured, showing an increase, indicating the limb is seven millimeters longer than the contralateral side. A trial reduction is performed, and the surgeon evaluates the pseudo-intraoperative x-ray, noting the limb length discrepancy.

The surgeon removes the trial implant and reams deeper into the femur before inserting the actual prosthetic device several millimeters deeper. The hip is reduced, checkpoints and arrays are removed, and the hip capsule is closed using a transosseous repair. The quadratus femoris is closed, followed by a layered closure from the fascia to the skin.
